name: Canada
metaTitle: E-2 Visa for Canadian Nationals | Watson Immigration Law
metaDescription: Canada is the second-largest source of E-2 investors. Guidance on the E-2 Treaty Investor Visa for Canadian nationals — USMCA treaty status, cross-border investment, consulate options, and how E-2 compares to TN visa.
intro: Canada is the second-largest source of E-2 Treaty Investor Visa holders, with approximately 6,800 visas issued annually. Canadian nationals have a long history of cross-border investment in the United States, and E-2 is one of several U.S. immigration pathways available to Canadians — alongside TN (under USMCA), E-1 (trade), and the standard immigrant visa routes. Watson Immigration Law advises Canadian investors on whether E-2 or another pathway best fits their specific situation.

sections.heading: Treaty Status & History
sections.body: Canada's E-2 treaty access stems from the Treaty of Friendship, Commerce and Navigation and was further reinforced through trade agreements including NAFTA (now USMCA). Canadian nationals have had consistent E-2 access since 1989. Unlike TN visas — which are tied to specific USMCA-listed occupations — the E-2 is available to any Canadian national making a qualifying investment in a U.S. business, regardless of their profession.

sections.heading: E-2 vs. TN Visa for Canadians
sections.body: Canadian investors often ask whether to pursue E-2 or TN status. The key distinction: TN is for Canadian professionals working for a U.S. employer in a listed USMCA occupation. E-2 is for Canadians who own and operate a U.S. business. If you are investing in and running your own U.S. company, E-2 is typically the correct visa. TN is an employment visa, not an investor visa. Many Canadian entrepreneurs start on TN status while employed, then transition to E-2 when they launch their own business.

sections.heading: Visa Validity for Canadian Nationals
sections.body: Canadian nationals typically receive a 5-year E-2 visa stamp. Entry admissions are for 2-year periods, renewable indefinitely. Canadians also benefit from being able to enter the U.S. visa-free at land borders and airports for B-1/B-2 purposes, which simplifies initial business visits before E-2 status is established.

sections.heading: Consulate Processing for Canadians
sections.body: Canadian nationals can apply for E-2 at U.S. consulates in Toronto, Vancouver, Calgary, or Montreal, among others. Canadians may also apply for a change of status within the United States if they are already lawfully present. Processing times vary by location — Vancouver and Toronto typically schedule interviews within 4–6 weeks. Canadians do not need a visa to enter the U.S. for short visits, but E-2 status is required for those intending to own and operate a business.

sections.heading: Cross-Border Investment Considerations
sections.body: Canadian investors frequently structure businesses on both sides of the border — a Canadian parent company with a U.S. subsidiary or affiliate. This structure can support E-2 eligibility if the Canadian investor controls and operates the U.S. entity. Watson Immigration Law advises on both the immigration and business structuring aspects of cross-border investment.

faqs.question: Do Canadian nationals qualify for the E-2 visa?
faqs.answer: Yes. Canada has had an E-2 treaty relationship with the United States since 1989, and Canadian nationals are the second-largest group of E-2 visa holders globally. Canadian nationals can apply at U.S. consulates in Canada or change status from within the United States.

faqs.question: How does E-2 compare to TN status for Canadians?
faqs.answer: TN is an employment visa for Canadians working in listed USMCA professional occupations for a U.S. employer. E-2 is an investor visa for Canadians who own and operate a U.S. business. If you are starting or buying a U.S. company, E-2 is the right category. TN does not allow self-employment or business ownership.

faqs.question: Can I apply for E-2 at a Canadian consulate or from within the U.S.?
faqs.answer: Both options are available to Canadians. If you are in Canada, you apply through a U.S. consulate (Toronto, Vancouver, Calgary, Montreal, etc.). If you are already in the U.S. with a valid nonimmigrant status, you may be able to file a change of status application with USCIS — though consular processing is often faster.

faqs.question: Can a Canadian company open a U.S. subsidiary and get E-2 status?
faqs.answer: Yes. Canadian nationals who own at least 50% of a U.S. subsidiary can apply for E-2 status to manage that U.S. entity. The investment made into the U.S. entity must be substantial, at risk, and from the investor's own funds (or funds they are responsible for).

faqs.question: Does my Canadian spouse get work authorization on E-2?
faqs.answer: Yes. E-2 dependent spouses of Canadian investors receive automatic U.S. work authorization. They can work for any employer without filing a separate EAD application. This is a significant advantage for Canadian families who want both partners to work in the United States.
